title: Quasinormal modes and continuum response of de Sitter black holes via complex scaling method
authors: Shoya Ogawa, Okuto Morikawa, Takuya Hirose
abstract: We apply the complex scaling method to black-hole perturbations in four-dimensional Schwarzschild--de~Sitter (dS) spacetimes. The method converts the outgoing-wave boundary-value problem into a non-Hermitian spectral problem and enables quasinormal-mode poles and the rotated continuum to be treated in a common framework. We focus in particular on the continuum level density, which characterizes the continuum response beyond isolated quasinormal-mode frequencies. Using Regge--Wheeler-type perturbation equations for scalar, electromagnetic, and gravitational fields, we investigate how a nonzero cosmological constant modifies the pole and continuum sectors. We also discuss a possible extension to string-inspired coupled-channel systems, and illustrate that higher-dimensional dS black holes can be treated within the same framework, at least in tensor- and vector-type sectors. Our results indicate that complex scaling offers a useful spectral framework for analyzing both quasinormal modes and continuum response in black-hole physics.
summary: This paper develops a unified spectral framework using the complex scaling method to study both the discrete resonances and the continuous spectrum of de Sitter black holes. It reveals how the cosmological constant influences the spectral density and shows that the lowest quasinormal mode dominates the continuum response. The method is also shown to be applicable to more complex, higher-dimensional, and string-inspired black hole models.
main_problem: The paper aims to extend the complex scaling method (CSM) to Schwarzschild-de Sitter spacetimes to investigate how a non-zero cosmological constant modifies both quasinormal-mode poles and the continuum spectral response.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that CSM provides a unified framework for treating QNM poles and the rotated continuum, showing that the lowest QNM pole is the primary driver of the continuum level density structure and that increasing the cosmological constant shifts these features toward lower frequencies.
method: The complex scaling method (CSM) is applied to Regge-Wheeler-type perturbation equations, using a numerical variational approach with a polynomial times real-range Gaussian basis expansion.
model_or_system: The study focuses on four-dimensional Schwarzschild-de Sitter black holes, including scalar, electromagnetic, and gravitational perturbations, as well as extensions to higher-dimensional (5D) and string-inspired (MSW) black hole models.
key_observables: Quasinormal mode (QNM) frequencies, continuum level density (CLD), transmission phase, and greybody factors.
important_parameters: Cosmological constant (Lambda), spin (s = 0, 1, 2), angular momentum (l = 2, 3), and the scaling angle (theta).
main_assumptions: The analysis focuses on the weakly curved regime (small Lambda) and assumes the validity of the Regge-Wheeler-type master equations in the de Sitter background.
figures_summary: Plots of the complex frequency plane showing discretized continua and QNM candidates; numerical tables of QNM frequencies; and series of plots showing the continuum level density (CLD) and its partial reconstruction using the lowest QNM poles.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the mathematical framework of CSM in dS spacetimes, proceeds to numerical implementation and validation against known Schwarzschild results, analyzes the dependence of QNMs and CLD on the cosmological constant, and concludes with extensions to higher-dimensional and coupled-channel systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
